72-hour-emergency-go-bag-(fema-/-ready.g: Water: 1 gallon per person per day (3-day minimum = 3 gallons/person); purification tablets or filter as backup
Food: 3-day supply non-perishable (no-cook options preferred: energy bars, nuts, jerky, canned goods with pop-tops)
Battery or hand-crank NOAA weather radio
Flashlight with spare batteries (or headlamp)
First aid kit (include bleed-control: tourniquet, pressure bandage, hemostatic gauze)
Whistle for signaling
Dust mask / N95 respirator
Plastic sheeting and duct tape (shelter-in-place capability)
Moist towelettes and hand sanitizer (sanitation)
Garbage bags and zip ties
Non-sparking wrench or pliers (utility shutoff)
Manual can opener
Local paper maps (phones and GPS fail)
Cell phone with chargers and backup power bank
Waterproof container with copies of vital documents (ID, insurance, contacts, prescriptions)
Cash in small bills
Prescription medications (7-day supply)
Extra glasses or contact lens supplies if needed
Infant or pet supplies if applicable
Emergency mylar blankets (one per person)
Change of clothing and sturdy closed-toe shoes per person
backcountry-/-wilderness-survival-kit-(t: Navigation: Topographic map of the area + baseplate compass (declination adjusted); GPS optional
Sun protection: Sunscreen SPF 30+, sunglasses, wide-brim hat
Insulation: Extra layers for the worst-case weather scenario; rain gear
Illumination: Headlamp with fresh batteries + spare batteries
First aid kit: Bleed control items, moleskin, SAM splint, irrigation syringe, WFA manual
Fire: Lighter + ferro rod + waterproof matches (three ignition methods); tinder (petroleum jelly cotton balls or wax-based starter)
Repair tools and knife: Fixed-blade knife or multi-tool, duct tape, paracord (50 ft minimum)
Hydration: Water filter or purification tablets; extra capacity container
Emergency shelter: Lightweight tarp or bivy + emergency mylar blanket
Signaling: PLB or Garmin inReach; signal mirror; loud whistle
Communication: Charged phone in dry bag; written emergency contact plan left with a trusted person
improvised-debris-hut-build-sequence: Select a site protected from wind, not in a drainage or low-lying cold air pool
Gather ridgepole: a stout straight branch approximately 9-12 feet long
Prop ridgepole at hip height on a fork, stump, or a bipod of crossed sticks
Angle ribs (branches) against both sides of the ridgepole to form an A-frame skeleton
Pile coarse debris (branches, sticks) over the ribs to form a lattice
Heap dry leaves, pine needles, ferns, or grass over the lattice: minimum 2 feet deep all around (more is better)
Create a door plug: a bundle of leaves stuffed into a garbage bag or packed into a mesh of sticks
Lay at least 4-6 inches of dry debris on the floor for insulation from ground conduction
Fill interior with armfuls of dry leaves to serve as a body-length sleeping bag
Test fit: inside should be just wide enough to roll over; excess space is warmth lost
Mark the location with a signal (bright material on top, visible from above)
stop-the-bleed:-hemorrhage-control-decis: Call 911 (or direct someone to call) immediately if possible
Identify the source of bleeding by exposing the wound
LIMB bleeding, severe: Apply tourniquet 2-3 inches above wound (not over joint), tighten windlass until bleeding stops, write time on tourniquet or skin
TORSO / JUNCTIONAL bleeding (groin, armpit, neck — tourniquet not applicable): Pack wound firmly with gauze pressing against bleeding source; apply sustained direct pressure minimum 3 minutes
SURFACE / LACERATION: Apply firm direct pressure with cleanest material available; do not release for at least 10 minutes
Maintain pressure until EMS arrives; do not remove a tourniquet once applied
Monitor patient for shock: lay flat, elevate legs if no spinal injury suspected, maintain warmth
Document time of tourniquet application and relay to EMS
survival-signaling-priority-sequence: Activate PLB or satellite communicator immediately if available and situation warrants
Stay in a visible location: open ground, hilltop, clearing, lakeside
Blow emergency whistle in groups of three at regular intervals
Prepare a signal fire in a clearing (three fires in a triangle if resources allow)
Construct ground-to-air symbol (V for help, X for medical) at least 10 feet tall in a clearing
Use signal mirror whenever aircraft or sunlight is present: aim reflected beam toward aircraft
Flash a light (three short, three long, three short = SOS) toward any visible lights at night
Do not move from your last known position unless movement is imperative for survival
72-hour-emergency-water-kit-(per-person): 3 gallons of water minimum stored in food-grade HDPE or PETE containers
Unscented household bleach (5–8.25% sodium hypochlorite) with dosage card
Eyedropper or measuring dropper for bleach dosing
Portable water filter rated to at least 0.2 microns (removes bacteria and protozoa)
Chemical disinfection tablets (chlorine dioxide preferred for broadest spectrum)
Collapsible or rigid water containers for collection if resupply needed
Coffee filters or clean cloth for pre-filtering turbid water
Water purification reference card (boil times, bleach doses)
14-day-food-storage-checklist-(1-adult,-: ~10 lbs white rice (~3,640 kcal, stored in sealed Mylar bag with O2 absorber)
~5 lbs dried beans or lentils (~2,720 kcal, protein source)
~3 lbs rolled oats (~5,300 kcal, breakfast staple)
16 oz peanut butter or nut butter (~2,700 kcal, fat and protein)
Multivitamin supply for 14 days (prevents micronutrient deficiency)
Canned vegetables and fruit (variety; vitamin C source)
Cooking oil (vegetable or olive; 1 quart minimum for calorie density)
Portable stove or camp stove with fuel for 14 days
water-purification-decision-tree-(field-: STEP 1: Is the water visibly turbid or cloudy? If yes, pre-filter through cloth or coffee filter first
STEP 2: Do you have fuel and a vessel? Boil 1 minute (3 min above 6,500 ft) — most reliable method
STEP 3: No fire available? Use chlorine dioxide tablets if available (broadest spectrum, works on Cryptosporidium with 4-hr wait)
STEP 4: No tablets? Use bleach — 8 drops per gallon (clear water), 16 drops (cloudy); wait 30 min
STEP 5: No bleach? Use iodine — 5 drops per quart clear, 10 drops cloudy; wait 30 min (60 min if cold)
STEP 6: Portable filter only? Add chemical treatment after filtering — most field filters do NOT remove viruses
STEP 7: No chemical, no fire? SODIS — clear PET bottle, <30 NTU turbidity, full sun 6 hours or 48 hours overcast
STEP 8: NEVER use floodwater with possible chemical contamination — use only bottled water
foraging-safety-pre-checklist: Carry at least two regional field guides and cross-reference before eating anything
Verify identification using multiple plant characteristics (leaf shape, stem, root, smell, habitat, season)
Learn the top 5–10 toxic species in your specific region before foraging
Do NOT forage within 50 meters of roads, railroads, or agricultural fields (contamination)
Do NOT assume safety because wildlife or livestock eat the plant
Do NOT rely on taste or smell alone — some deadly plants are mild or pleasant-tasting
Prepare foraged food correctly — some safe species require cooking (elderberries, cattail pollen, nettles)
If in doubt, do not eat it — caloric deficit for 1–2 days is safer than plant poisoning
Have Poison Control number accessible: US 1-800-222-1222
dehydration-self-assessment-field-checkl: Check urine color: pale yellow = well hydrated; dark yellow/amber = mild to moderate dehydration; no output = severe
Mild symptoms (thirst, headache, fatigue): increase fluid intake, add electrolytes if available
Moderate symptoms (dizziness, confusion, rapid heart rate): cease heavy activity, rest in shade, ORS solution
Do NOT attempt to 'catch up' rapidly — drink steadily 250–500 mL at a time
Monitor for hyponatremia if drinking large volumes of plain water during prolonged exertion: symptoms include nausea, headache, confusion, seizure — add electrolytes, do NOT give more plain water
Severe dehydration with altered mental status requires evacuation and IV fluids — field first aid is supportive only
march-primary-survey-(tactical-field-car: M — Check for life-threatening extremity bleeding: apply tourniquet 2-3 inches above wound, tighten until bleeding stops, note T-time
M — Check for junctional/truncal bleeding: pack wound with hemostatic gauze, apply firm pressure for 3+ minutes
A — Assess airway: if unconscious and breathing, place in recovery position
A — If airway compromised: insert NPA (28Fr) lubricated, right nostril, directed along floor of nasal cavity
A — If NPA fails and airway obstructed: prepare for surgical cricothyroidotomy
R — Assess breathing: look/listen/feel for chest rise
R — Identify open chest wound: seal immediately with vented chest seal (both entry and exit wounds)
R — Assess for tension pneumothorax: absent breath sounds + worsening shock = needle decompression at ICS2-MCL
C — Assess for shock: altered mental status, weak/absent radial pulse
C — Establish IV/IO access if trained; administer fluids per protocol
C — Reassess all hemorrhage controls: tourniquet still effective? Pressure dressing soaked through?
H — Remove wet clothing, place insulation under and over casualty
H — Apply space blanket reflective-side-in, cover head
H — Place chemical heat packs in axillae and groin if available
H — Assess for TBI: check GCS, pupils equal and reactive, motor function in all four limbs
H — If TBI suspected: prevent hypoxia and hypotension; avoid hyperventilation
tourniquet-application-checklist: Expose the wound to identify bleeding site
Apply tourniquet 2-3 inches proximal to the wound (NOT over a joint)
Route the strap through the friction buckle and pull extremely tight — tighter than feels right
Engage the windlass rod and twist until bright-red bleeding stops AND distal pulse is eliminated
Lock the windlass rod in the clip/holder
Write the time of application (T-time) on the white label or on the casualty's forehead with marker
Do NOT cover the tourniquet — it must be visible for medical handoff
Do NOT remove or loosen in the field — hospital decision only
If bleeding continues: apply a second tourniquet proximal to the first; do not simply retighten the first
wound-packing-with-hemostatic-gauze: Apply direct pressure briefly to assess bleeding and locate the source
Open hemostatic gauze (QuikClot Combat Gauze or Celox); do not contaminate
Pack gauze DIRECTLY into the wound cavity, pressing down to the deepest point of bleeding
Continue packing until the cavity is filled — do not leave air pockets
Apply firm, sustained direct pressure with both hands for a minimum of 3 minutes (do not release and check)
Apply pressure bandage (Israeli bandage) over the packed wound to maintain compression
Reassess: if bleeding has not stopped, repack with additional gauze
Mark casualty card or forehead with time and type of hemostatic agent used
standard-ifak-packing-checklist: Tourniquet x1 (CAT Gen 7 or SOF-T Wide) — on exterior of kit or on body for one-hand access
Hemostatic gauze x1-2 (QuikClot Combat Gauze or Celox Gauze)
Israeli bandage / Emergency Trauma Dressing x1 (4-6 inch)
Vented chest seals x2 (Hyfin, HALO, or SAM chest seal) — paired for entry and exit wounds
Casualty Card (DD Form 1380 or commercial equivalent)
Optional: SAM splint, wound closure strips, penlight, personal medications
heat-stroke-emergency-response: Recognize: hot skin (may or may not be sweating) + altered mental status + temperature above 104F (40C)
Move casualty to shade or cool environment
COOL AGGRESSIVELY: cold-water immersion is most effective; if unavailable, soak skin with water and fan vigorously
Apply ice packs to neck, axillae (armpits), and groin — high blood-flow areas
Do NOT give aspirin, acetaminophen, or NSAIDs — they are not effective for heat stroke and may worsen liver/kidney injury
If alert and not vomiting: small sips of cool water or electrolyte solution
Continue cooling until core temperature reaches 102F (38.9C) or mental status improves
Monitor airway — seizures and vomiting risk
Do NOT delay transport — evacuate emergently to definitive care
hypothermia-prevention-in-trauma-(field): Begin hypothermia prevention immediately — do not wait until care is otherwise complete
Remove wet clothing if operationally safe and feasible
Place insulation UNDER the casualty before anything else (ground conduction is the largest heat loss route)
Wrap with space/mylar blanket, reflective side facing the body
Cover the head and neck (up to 40-50% of heat loss is cephalic)
Place chemical heat packs in axillae and groin — do NOT place directly on skin; wrap in a layer of clothing
Seal the wrap to prevent convective losses from wind
Warm IV fluids if in Prolonged Field Care and equipment is available
Monitor for cardiac arrhythmia in severe hypothermia (below 28C / 82.4F)
Treat gently — cold hearts are prone to ventricular fibrillation with rough handling
pre-ruck-preparation-checklist: Weigh pack with all contents before stepping off — know your exact load
Confirm load is 10% BW (beginner) or within your trained range
Heaviest items positioned high and against the back panel
Hip belt seated on iliac crest, not the waist; tighten until load transfers to hips
Shoulder straps adjusted so no gap between strap and shoulder top
Load lifter straps at approximately 45-degree angle (if present)
Pack does not sag or bounce when walking
Toenails trimmed straight and short
Calluses and rough nail edges filed smooth
Anti-chafe balm or petroleum jelly applied to heels, ball of foot, between toes
Liner sock (synthetic) worn first; boot sock turned inside-out over it
Pre-tape known hotspot areas with moleskin or Leukotape
Gold Bond or moisture-absorbing powder in socks (optional)
Boots are fully broken in (40+ miles prior wear)
Hydration bladder filled (2–3L minimum); electrolyte tabs or packets packed
Pre-march meal eaten 2–3 hours prior (carbs + protein, low fat/fiber)
16–20 oz water consumed 2 hours before; 8 oz within 30 min of start
Snacks accessible in hip-belt or top-lid pockets (not buried in main compartment)
Spare dry socks in accessible pocket or top lid
Moleskin, pins, antibiotic ointment, and bandages in field first aid kit
Weather and terrain appropriate footwear selected
during-the-ruck-\u2014-form-and-pacing-c: Posture: shoulders back, core engaged, forward gaze (not feet)
Stride: shorter and quicker rather than long heel-strike steps
Foot contact: aim for mid-foot landing, not heel strike
On hills: slightly increase lean and shorten stride; do not bounce
On downhill: shorten stride, mild knee bend, slow pace to protect quads and knees
Drink 16–24 oz water per hour; do not wait for thirst
Add electrolytes every 1–2 hours on events over 90 minutes
Consume 30–60g carbohydrate per hour on events over 90 minutes
At first hotspot sensation on foot: stop, remove boot, re-sock or tape immediately
Change into dry socks at halts on marches over 6 miles
Check hip belt and shoulder strap adjustment every 3–4 miles — straps loosen with sweat
Note any sharp bone pain, joint swelling, or numbness — these are stop signals
progressive-training-safety-rules: Increase only ONE variable per week: distance, weight, or pace — never all three
Maximum 10–20% increase per week in any single variable
Ruck 2–3 days per week (beginner); no more than 4 days/week (advanced)
Minimum 1 full rest day between ruck sessions
Deload every 4th week: reduce distance and weight 30–40%
Add strength training 2–3x per week: focus on posterior chain, core, glutes
Do not ruck on consecutive days until conditioned (8+ weeks consistent training)
If shin pain develops: reduce volume 30–50%, move to soft surfaces, no increase for 2 weeks
If knee pain develops: stop downhill segments, reduce weight, evaluate quad/hamstring strength
If lower back pain develops: check pack fit, reduce weight, add core stability work
Seek medical evaluation for: localized sharp bone pain, swelling, inability to weight-bear, worsening pain during activity
field-blister-treatment-protocol: Small intact blister: do NOT pop
Cut a doughnut hole in moleskin centered over blister to offload pressure
Apply doughnut over blister; cover with second moleskin or bandage layer
Large painful blister preventing walking: sterilize needle/pin with alcohol flame or alcohol wipe
Drain fluid gently by pressing from sides — leave skin roof intact
Apply antibiotic ointment (bacitracin, triple antibiotic) to roof and surrounding skin
Cover with non-stick gauze or bandage; layer moleskin doughnut over top
Change dressing at next halt; keep clean and dry
Seek sick call / medical if: spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks from site, fever, or failure to improve in 24h
pre-mission-map-preparation: Obtain the correct map sheet(s) covering your entire route plus buffer
Check the map's edition date and datum (NAD27, NAD83, or WGS84)
Note the contour interval and bar scale in the map margin
Read the declination diagram: record the G-M angle (grid to magnetic) and the date
Verify current declination at NOAA calculator (ngdc.noaa.gov/geomag) if map is old
Mark your start point, objective, checkpoints, and attack point in pencil
Draw route legs and record the azimuth and distance for each leg
Identify key terrain features and obstacles along each leg
Note any avenues of approach and deliberate offset direction near the objective
Cover or laminate the map if wet conditions are expected
navigation-kit-checklist: Topographic map of the area (1:25,000 or 1:24,000 preferred)
Compass (lensatic or baseplate, with a working declination setting)
Protractor or roamer matching your map scale
Pencil and waterproof notebook for recording legs, pace counts, observations
Ranger beads (pace count beads) or wrist counter
GPS device or smartphone with offline maps downloaded (CalTopo/Gaia GPS)
Spare batteries or backup power for electronics
Printed paper map as backup even if using GPS
Signal device (whistle, mirror, PLB) for emergency use
Red-lens flashlight or headlamp for night navigation
compass-to-cheek-azimuth-procedure-(lens: Open the compass cover to approximately 90 degrees from the base
Set the eyepiece (rear sight) to approximately 45 degrees
Hold the compass at eye level with the sighting wire aligned toward the target
Look through the eyepiece lens and align the sighting wire with your target
While maintaining alignment, read the bearing from the rotating drum beneath the index line
Note the azimuth; if using magnetic bearing on a map with declination, convert as required
Verify by repeating the sighting — minor variations are normal; average three readings for precision
resection-(finding-your-position-with-a-: Identify two or more distinct landmarks visible on the ground AND on your map
Shoot an azimuth to Landmark 1; record the magnetic azimuth
Draw a line on the map through Landmark 1's map symbol at the back-azimuth angle
The point where the lines intersect is your position
With three lines, your position is inside the triangle of error; pick the centroid
Confirm by checking nearby terrain features match your estimated position
pace-count-calibration-procedure: Measure a 100-meter baseline on ground representative of your mission terrain
Put on the load you will carry in the field
Start with feet together; step off on left foot
Count each time the LEFT foot strikes the ground (one pace = two steps)
Walk the baseline three times; record each count
Average the three runs — this is your 100m pace count
Add 10-20 paces for steep uphill legs; increase for night, mud, snow, or heavy vegetation
Verify pace count against known distances at checkpoints during the mission
oakoc-terrain-analysis-checklist: O — Observation: What can be seen from key positions? Identify dead ground and masking terrain
A — Avenues of Approach: What corridors allow movement toward the objective? Grade, width, surface?
K — Key Terrain: What ground provides decisive advantage if controlled? Hilltops, bridges, chokepoints?
O — Obstacles: What stops or channels movement? Rivers, cliffs, dense vegetation, man-made barriers?
C — Cover and Concealment: What provides protection (cover) or hides movement (concealment)?
Assess how each factor affects YOUR movement
Assess how each factor affects any opposing force or hazard
Integrate weather effects (visibility, ground conditions) into each factor
go-kit-/-field-radio-kit: Primary radio: battery charged, programmed, external antenna attached or stored
Alternate radio (different service/band): charged and programmed
Contingency device (HF radio, sat messenger, or LoRa mesh node)
USB programming cable + CHIRP installed on laptop or Chromebook
Laminated backup channel/frequency card (does not need power)
PACE plan printed — one copy per team member
Repeater CTCSS/DCS tones verified against RepeaterBook or myGMRS
Simplex backup frequencies programmed alongside repeater channels
Spare batteries or alkaline battery tray for each radio
Solar panel or power bank (minimum 10,000 mAh) for recharging
Headset/earpiece with PTT for covert or high-noise environments
Roll-up J-pole or telescoping whip antenna for VHF/UHF improvement
For HF: lightweight dipole wire, center insulator, and 50-ohm coax
Frequency reference card (FRS/GMRS channels, ham bands, MURS)
pace-plan-verification-checklist: All four tiers (P/A/C/E) defined and written down
Each tier uses independent infrastructure (different bands, frequencies, or technologies)
Trigger conditions defined: when to shift from P to A, A to C, C to E
Schedule defined: check-in times, guard frequencies, and listening windows
All team members have received and read the plan
Plan rehearsed at least once before deployment or event
Authentication challenge/response defined if security matters
Battery/power contingency addressed for each tier
Alternative to PACE (runner, visual signals) defined as absolute last resort
getting-licensed-(ham-radio)-checklist: Choose entry level: Technician for local VHF/UHF; plan for General if HF is needed
Study at HamStudy.org (https://hamstudy.org) — free, covers current question pool
Practice at HamExam.org (https://www.hamexam.org) with timed mock exams
Score consistently 80%+ on practice exams before scheduling the real test
Find an exam session at https://hamstudy.org/sessions — search by ZIP code
Bring: government-issued ID, FCC FRN number (register free at https://www.fcc.gov/wireless/support/universal-licensing-system/getting-fcc-registration-number-frn), exam fee (~$15–35 cash)
After passing: FCC posts license to ULS in 1–2 business days — do NOT transmit until callsign appears in FCC database
Pay the $35 FCC application fee at https://www.fcc.gov within 10 days of exam
Once licensed: join local ARES group via https://www.arrl.org/ares
Program your radio using CHIRP and a local repeater from RepeaterBook
radio-net-operating-quick-reference: Listen before transmitting — confirm channel is clear
Identify yourself by call sign at start and end of transmission (required by FCC for ham)
Use NATO phonetic alphabet for all letters, call signs, and grid coordinates
SAY AGAIN to request a repeat (not REPEAT on a military/tactical net)
ROGER = received only; WILCO = received and will comply; never say ROGER WILCO
OVER = expecting reply; OUT = conversation ended; never use both together
Keep transmissions concise — think before keying the mic
Use minimum power needed for reliable communication
On a directed net, wait for Net Control Station to call you before transmitting
BREAK to interrupt for urgent/emergency traffic
For emergency call: MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY (distress) or BREAK BREAK BREAK (urgent) on any monitored frequency
dave-canterbury's-10-cs-of-survivability: Combustion device (BIC lighter + ferro rod backup)
Cover/shelter (wool blanket, tarp, or emergency bivy)
Container (32 oz stainless steel wide-mouth bottle)
Cotton bandana (multi-use: filter, sun protection, tinder char cloth)
Cloth sail needle (gear and clothing repair)
Cargo tape (duct tape or Gorilla tape for repairs)
10-essentials-\u2014-modern-systems-appr: Navigation: topo map (waterproofed) + baseplate compass + GPS or satellite communicator
Sun protection: SPF 30+ sunscreen + UV sunglasses + wide-brim hat
Insulation: extra layers beyond forecast minimum (puffy, rain jacket, hat, gloves)
First-aid supplies: kit appropriate to trip duration + blister care + wound closure
Fire starter: primary lighter + ferro rod + dry tinder
Repair kit and tools: knife/multi-tool + duct tape + patches + safety pins
Nutrition: extra food (200+ cal/day buffer) — non-perishable
Hydration: 2-liter carrying capacity + filter (Sawyer Squeeze) + chemical backup (Micropur)
Emergency shelter: space blanket, bivy, or silnylon tarp
core-edc-kit-(on-person-tier-1): Folding knife (3-inch blade, quality lock mechanism)
Pocket or keychain flashlight (100–300 lumens, common battery)
All-weather pen (Fisher Space Pen or Rite in the Rain)
Wallet: cash (small bills) + ID + emergency contact card
Tourniquet (CAT or SOFTT-W) — at minimum in day pack
ifak-/-trauma-kit-minimum-contents: Hemostatic gauze (QuikClot Combat Gauze or Celox)
Vented chest seal x2 (Hyfin or Russell)
Reference card: Stop the Bleed / MARCH protocol
72-hour-emergency-go-bag-(fema-ready.gov: Water: 1 gallon per person per day (3 days = 3 gallons/person)
Food: 3-day supply of non-perishable, ready-to-eat items
Battery or hand-crank NOAA weather radio
Cell phone + charger + backup battery bank
Sleeping bag or warm blanket per person
footwear-selection-decision-matrix: Heavy load (>30 lbs) OR technical terrain: use hiking boots with high ankle collar
Moderate load, moderate terrain: mid-cut hiking shoes (compromise)
Light load, established trails, thru-hike: trail running shoes
Cold/wet sustained conditions: waterproof boots (GORE-TEX lined) + moisture-wicking socks
Always: avoid cotton socks — use merino wool or synthetic
Multi-day trips: bring 2 pairs of socks, dry one while wearing the other
Blister prevention: liner sock under outer sock, Leukotape on known hot spots before they blister
Break in new footwear with planned socks on similar terrain before field use
ecwcs-gen-iii-layer-selection-guide: Level 1 (Polartec Power Dry): wicking base, mild-cold weather or high exertion
Level 2 (Midweight base): cold weather base, adds insulation
Level 3 (Fleece jacket/trousers): primary cold-weather insulation layer
Level 4 (Wind jacket): add when wind is present, breathable
Level 5 (Soft shell): active movement in moderate/cold/dry conditions
Level 6 (GORE-TEX hard shell): rain/wet/wind outer protection
Level 7 (Extreme cold parka): static or extreme cold (-20°F and below)
COLD mnemonic: Keep it Clean, Avoid Overheating, Loose and in Layers, Keep it Dry
72-hour-go-bag:-core-items: Water: 1 gallon per person (minimum 3-day supply; supplement with purification tablets or filter)
Non-perishable food: 3-day supply, no-cook preferred (canned goods, energy bars, dried fruit, jerky, peanut butter, crackers)
Manual can opener
Battery-powered or hand-crank NOAA Weather Radio with tone alert
Flashlight with extra batteries (or hand-crank/solar model)
First aid kit (bandages, antiseptic wipes, antibiotic ointment, gauze, medical tape, gloves, CPR face shield, tweezers, scissors, thermometer, aspirin/ibuprofen/acetaminophen)
Tourniquet (CAT or SOFTT-W type; take a Stop the Bleed class to learn use)
Whistle (to signal for help if trapped)
N95 or dust mask per person
Plastic sheeting and duct tape (shelter-in-place sealing)
Moist towelettes, garbage bags, plastic ties (sanitation)
Wrench or pliers (to shut off gas and water utilities)
Printed local maps (do not rely on phone GPS alone)
Cell phone with chargers and external battery bank
Prescription medications: minimum 7-day supply in original labeled containers
Copies of important documents in waterproof container (ID, insurance, medical records, contact list)
Cash in small bills (ATMs may be offline)
Sleeping bag or emergency thermal blanket per person
Change of clothes and sturdy closed-toe shoes per person
Glasses/contacts and supplies if needed
Infant formula, diapers, wipes if applicable
Pet supplies: food, water, medications, carrier, records, leash, ID collar
extended-home-cache-(2-week-supply)-addi: Water: 14 gallons per person (or commercial 5-gallon water jugs plus purification capability)
Food: 14-day supply at 2,000+ calories/adult/day (rice, dried beans, pasta, canned vegetables, canned protein, cooking oil, salt, spices)
Camp stove or propane burner with extra fuel canisters (outdoor use only)
Cooler and ice packs (food safety during power outage)
Portable generator with 5+ gallons of fuel (stored safely in approved containers outdoors)
Heavy-duty outdoor-rated extension cords for generator use
Carbon monoxide detector (battery-operated; required if generator or combustion heat is used)
Extra prescription medications (request 90-day fills; discuss emergency supply with provider)
Sanitation: portable toilet or 5-gallon bucket with snap-on seat, waste bags, cat litter or sawdust, hand sanitizer, extra bleach
Extra batteries in all common sizes (AA, AAA, D, 9V)
Extra fuel for vehicles (stored in approved containers with fuel stabilizer)
Entertainment and morale: books, games, playing cards, comfort items for children
Work gloves and eye protection (for debris cleanup)
water-purification-quick-reference: BOILING: Bring to a rolling boil for 1 minute (3 minutes above 6,500 ft). Let cool before drinking.
BLEACH (clear water): 1/8 teaspoon (8 drops) of 5.25-6% unscented sodium hypochlorite bleach per gallon. Mix, wait 30 minutes. Should smell faintly of chlorine.
BLEACH (cloudy water): 1/4 teaspoon (16 drops) per gallon. Filter through clean cloth first.
If no chlorine odor after 30 minutes: repeat dose, wait 15 more minutes. If still no odor, discard water.
Do NOT use: scented bleach, color-safe bleach, or bleach with added cleaners.
Portable ceramic or hollow-fiber filter: effective for Giardia, Cryptosporidium (not viruses alone).
Iodine tablets: follow package directions; avoid if pregnant, thyroid condition, or long-term use.
Replace stored non-commercial water every 6 months.
Store containers away from petroleum products; plastic absorbs vapors.
family-communication-plan-(key-elements-: Designate one out-of-area contact: name, phone number, email (everyone memorizes this number)
Local meeting point #1: just outside home (example: mailbox, neighbor's house)
Local meeting point #2: outside neighborhood if you cannot return home (library, school, community center)
Out-of-area meeting destination if regional evacuation needed (relative's address, specific hotel chain)
Confirm how children will be picked up from school and who is an authorized alternate pickup
Know your workplace or school emergency protocols
Agree on primary and backup communication methods (text first, then calls, then email)
All household members carry physical wallet card with contact info (Ready.gov template: ready.gov/sites/default/files/2025-06/family-communication-plan_fillable-card.pdf)
Sign up for local Wireless Emergency Alerts (confirm cell phones are WEA-enabled; default on most US phones)
Sign up for county opt-in alert system (text/email) at your county emergency management website
Download FEMA App and configure alerts for your location and up to 4 other locations
Know your evacuation zone designation (if applicable) and designated state evacuation routes
power-outage-safety-checklist: NEVER use generator, grill, or camp stove indoors or in a garage — minimum 20 feet from windows
Install battery-operated CO detectors on every home level and outside sleeping areas
Unplug major appliances when power fails (protect against surge when power returns)
Leave one lamp plugged in as an indicator that power has returned
Keep refrigerator closed: safe approx. 4 hours; freezer safe approx. 48 hours (full, door closed)
Discard food above 40°F (4°C) for more than 2 hours
Use flashlights instead of candles; if candles necessary, never leave unattended
Know how to manually release your garage door opener
Check on neighbors — especially elderly, disabled, or those with medical equipment
If on power-dependent medical equipment, contact provider and utility company in advance for backup plan
Keep vehicle fuel tank at least half-full at all times in storm-prone seasons
important-documents-to-safeguard-(effak-: Birth certificates and Social Security cards
Health insurance cards and policy documents
Home/renters insurance and auto insurance policies
Mortgage or lease agreements and property deed
Bank and investment account numbers and contact info
Medical records and current medication list (prescriptions + dosages)
Will, trust documents, power of attorney
Emergency contact list (printed, not only on phone)
Veterinary records and vaccination documentation for pets
Storage location: fireproof/waterproof home safe PLUS one offsite copy (safety deposit box, trusted person, or encrypted cloud)
locating-a-public-army-doctrine-manual: Use the search bar or navigate to ProductMaps (FM, ATP, TC, ADP tabs)
Enter the publication number (e.g., TC 3-21.76, FM 3-0, ADP 6-22)
Check the Distribution Statement on the cover: Statement A = unlimited public release
Download the PDF directly from the APD link (free, no registration required)
For older or archived manuals, check archive.org or irp.fas.org/doddir/army/
Verify the publication date — doctrine is revised; always confirm you have the current edition
five-paragraph-operations-order-(smeac)-: 1. SITUATION: Enemy forces (composition, disposition, capabilities), friendly forces (higher unit mission, adjacent units), attachments/detachments
2. MISSION: Who, What, When, Where, Why — task and purpose stated in one sentence
3. EXECUTION: Commander's intent, concept of operations, tasks to each subordinate unit, coordinating instructions (actions on contact, ROE, consolidation plan)
4. SERVICE AND SUPPORT (also called Administration and Logistics): Sustainment plan, medical evacuation, resupply, prisoner handling
5. COMMAND AND SIGNAL: Chain of command, location of commander, communication plan (frequencies, call signs, challenge/password)
Issue Warning Order as soon as mission is received — do not wait for complete plan
Use terrain model or map overlay to brief; confirm all subordinates understand commander's intent
troop-leading-procedures-(tlp)-\u2014-sm: 1. Receive the Mission — analyze higher unit order
2. Issue a Warning Order — alert subordinates immediately so they can begin preparation
3. Make a Tentative Plan — apply METT-TC (Mission, Enemy, Terrain & weather, Troops available, Time, Civilians)
4. Initiate Movement — if required; don't wait for complete plan
5. Conduct Reconnaissance — leader recon of objective and routes if time permits
6. Complete the Plan — refine based on recon results
7. Issue the Order — full OPORD to all subordinates
8. Supervise — rehearsals, precombat checks, precombat inspections (PCIs)
mett-tc-mission-analysis-factors: M — Mission: What is the task and purpose?
E — Enemy: Composition, disposition, strength, capabilities, most likely/dangerous course of action
T — Terrain and Weather: Key terrain, observation and fields of fire, avenues of approach, cover and concealment, obstacles; weather effects
T — Troops available: What friendly forces are available; their training, readiness, and attachments
T — Time: How much time is available for planning, movement, execution, and consolidation?
C — Civilians: Civilian presence on the battlefield; legal constraints; information environment
key-public-doctrine-publications-to-know: ADP 1 — The Army (identity, ethos, Army culture)
ADP 6-22 — Army Leadership and the Profession
FM 3-0 — Operations (most current large-scale combat / multidomain operations doctrine)
ATP 3-21.8 — Infantry Platoon and Squad (primary small-unit tactics reference)
TC 3-21.76 — Ranger Handbook (field-condensed reference: patrol, navigation, survival, first aid, combat orders)
FM 3-25.26 / ATP 3-25.26 — Map Reading and Land Navigation
offline-field-device-preparation-checkli: Download OsmAnd or Organic Maps regional map packs for all areas of operation
Import waypoints, routes, and base camp locations as GPX files
Configure CHIRP with local repeaters, NOAA weather freqs, GMRS/FRS calling, and simplex emergency channels; write to radio
Download Kiwix ZIM files: full Wikipedia, WikiHow first-aid articles, relevant field manual PDFs
Load ATAK-CIV or CivTAK; configure team CoT server address or enable peer-to-peer mesh
Configure Meshtastic nodes with shared channel key, device names, and team contact list
Download FEMA hazard information sheets and TCCC Quick Reference Guide as PDF to device
Export QGIS project with offline raster tiles to QField for any survey or assessment work
Verify GPSBabel can read/write your device format; pre-load any required waypoints
Test all apps in airplane mode before departure
tccc-/-stop-the-bleed-response-prioritie: M — Massive hemorrhage: apply tourniquet or wound packing to life-threatening bleeding first
A — Airway: ensure airway is open; use recovery position or nasopharyngeal airway if trained
R — Respiration: seal penetrating chest wounds; check for tension pneumothorax if trained
C — Circulation: treat shock, establish IV/IO access if trained
H — Hypothermia/Head injury: prevent heat loss; document neuro status
Evacuate to higher care as rapidly as possible
Full formal TCCC training available through NAEMT (naemt.org) and military/first-responder courses
open-source-radio-/-comms-stack-\u2014-s: Baofeng UV-5R or UV-82 (or equivalent dual-band HT) programmed via CHIRP
CHIRP-compatible USB cable stored with radio
RTL-SDR v3 dongle + laptop with GQRX for spectrum monitoring and signal reception
Meshtastic LoRa node (e.g., Heltec LoRa 32 v3 or LilyGO T-Beam) for encrypted text mesh
Meshtastic Android or iOS app paired to node via Bluetooth
Printed frequency reference card: local repeaters, NOAA weather, aviation guard (121.5 MHz), marine ch 16
Backup battery / solar charger sized for multi-day operation
kiwix-offline-content-priority-download-: Wikipedia (English, no-pic version ~20 GB; full with pics ~90 GB)
WikiHow (all-languages, ~5 GB — procedures for first aid, tools, skills)
Khan Academy (video-free version for offline education)
Offline versions of relevant field manuals (load as PDF via any viewer, not ZIM)
9-line-medevac-request-(lines-1-9): Line 1: Location of the pickup site (grid or coordinates)
Line 2: Radio frequency, call sign, and suffix of requesting unit
Line 3: Number of patients by precedence (Urgent / Urgent-Surgical / Priority / Routine / Convenience)
Line 4: Special equipment required (hoist, extraction, ventilator, etc.)
Line 5: Number of patients by type (litter vs. ambulatory)
Line 6 (Wartime): Security of pickup site (No enemy / Possible enemy / Enemy in area / Enemy in area — armed escort required); (Peacetime): Number and type of wound, injury, or illness
Line 7: Method of marking pickup site (panels, pyro, smoke, lights, none)
Line 8: Patient nationality and status (US military, US civilian, non-US military, EPW, etc.)
Line 9: NBC contamination status (Nuclear / Biological / Chemical) or, if none, other critical information
march-trauma-assessment-sequence-(tccc): M — Massive Hemorrhage: Apply tourniquet high and tight on limb injuries; pack junctional wounds with hemostatic gauze and apply pressure
A — Airway: Position unconscious but breathing patient; insert NPA if needed; consider surgical airway for complete obstruction
R — Respiration: Check for tension pneumothorax; apply vented chest seal to open chest wounds; perform NCD if tension pneumothorax suspected
C — Circulation: IV or IO access; give TXA within 3 hours if hemorrhagic shock; reassess all tourniquets and wound packing
H — Hypothermia: Cover patient with a heat-reflective blanket; prevent heat loss from ground contact; warm fluids if available
salute-enemy-contact-report: S — Size: How many personnel, vehicles, aircraft
A — Activity: What are they doing (moving, emplacing, observing)
L — Location: Grid or description of where they were observed
U — Unit/Uniform: Identifying insignia, uniform, or equipment
T — Time: When the observation was made (DTG)
E — Equipment: Weapons, vehicles, communications gear observed
stop-survival-decision-cycle: Stop: Halt all movement. Do not travel further until calm
Think: Assess your situation — physical, environmental, resource
Observe: Survey surroundings — terrain, water, shelter material, hazards, landmarks
Plan: Formulate a prioritized action plan (first aid, shelter, fire, signaling, water, food)
individual-first-aid-kit-(ifak)-\u2014-t: CAT or SOFTT-W limb tourniquet (one minimum, ideally two)
Hemostatic gauze (Combat Gauze or equivalent) — for wound packing
pace-communications-/-actions-plan-templ: P — Primary: Primary method (e.g., cell phone / main radio net / primary route)
A — Alternate: Alternate method if primary fails (e.g., text / secondary radio freq / alternate route)
C — Contingency: Fallback for both failures (e.g., landline / runner / tertiary route)
E — Emergency: Last resort when all others fail (e.g., preset rally point / emergency beacon / known safe house)
72-hour-go-bag-(bob)-core-contents: Water: minimum 1 liter per person per day; water filter or purification tablets
Food: 3 days of calorie-dense non-perishables (nuts, bars, freeze-dried)
IFAK: tourniquet, pressure bandage, hemostatic gauze, nitrile gloves, CPR mask
Navigation: paper map of local area, compass
Shelter: emergency mylar blankets, small tarp or bivy
Fire: lighter, waterproof matches, ferrocerium rod
Communication: hand-crank or battery radio (NOAA weather), charged backup battery
Documents: copies of ID, insurance, emergency contacts in a waterproof bag
Cash: small bills for use if electronic payment systems are down
Clothing: change of clothes appropriate to local climate, rain gear
personal-cold-weather-gear-inspection-(b: Base layer: moisture-wicking synthetic or wool — NO cotton
Mid layer: insulating fleece or synthetic fill; dry and clean
Extremity protection: insulated gloves/mittens, wool/synthetic socks (carry a dry spare pair)
Head/neck: balaclava or insulating hat covering ears; neck gaiter
Boots: waterproof, insulated for expected temperature; broken in (no blisters)
Hand and foot chemical warmers as backup
Vapor barrier layer if expected temperature below −20°F
Emergency bivouac material: space blanket, bivy sack, or sleeping bag
High-calorie food and warm drink capability (vacuum flask)
heat-injury-prevention-checklist-(daily-: Check WBGT index before starting operations; apply work/rest table
Adjust WBGT: +5°F for rucksack or body armor; +10°F for full MOPP gear
Verify all personnel have adequate water (minimum 0.5 qt/hour at moderate exertion)
Do NOT exceed 1.5 qt/hour — hyponatremia risk
Brief personnel on heat injury signs: confusion, stopping sweating, hot/dry red skin = EMERGENCY
Buddy system: observe each other for stumbling, confusion, behavior change
Identify cooling resources: shade, water source, cold water immersion capability
Ensure high-heat casualty kit: ice packs or cold water container, spray bottle
Acclimatization: do not conduct high-intensity operations with unacclimatized personnel in first 10–14 days
Identify nearest medical facility and evacuation route before operations begin
altitude-ascent-safety-checklist: Know baseline susceptibility (previous AMS = higher risk)
Plan ascent: above 3,000 m, limit sleeping altitude gain to 300–500 m/day
Schedule a rest day every 1,000 m gained above 3,000 m
Apply 'climb high, sleep low' when acclimatizing
Carry acetazolamide (Diamox) if prescribed; begin 1–2 days before ascent
No alcohol or sedatives at altitude
Carry supplemental oxygen and/or Gamow bag for objectives above 4,000 m
Know and brief evacuation route before ascending
Perform daily tandem gait test (straight-line walking) to screen for HACE
Know HAPE warning signs: dry cough, dyspnea at rest, pink frothy sputum — DESCEND IMMEDIATELY
frostbite/hypothermia-field-treatment-qu: Remove casualty from cold/wind; get into best available shelter
Replace all wet clothing with dry; insulate from ground (never place directly on cold ground)
Handle gently — rough movement can trigger cardiac arrhythmia in moderate/severe hypothermia
Warm sweet drinks ONLY if casualty is fully conscious and able to swallow
For frostbitten extremity: do NOT rewarm unless guaranteed it will stay thawed
Rewarm frostbite in 37–39°C water for ~30 minutes; do NOT rub or apply direct heat
Give NSAIDs (ibuprofen) for frostbite pain and anti-thromboxane effect
Do NOT break frostbite blisters unless necessary for function
Do NOT give alcohol (accelerates heat loss)
Evacuate all but the mildest cases to definitive medical care
Severe hypothermia: begin CPR if pulseless — 'no one is dead until warm and dead'
water-survival-/-cold-water-immersion-re: Always wear PFD when boating in cold water — you cannot don it after entering cold shock
If immersed: control breathing for first 60 seconds — cold shock passes
Attempt immediate self-rescue within first 10 minutes before muscle incapacitation
If rescue is coming: assume H.E.L.P. position (arms crossed, knees up) if wearing PFD
Multiple survivors: form huddle — chests together, arms around each other
Do NOT remove clothing in the water — it provides insulation
Once rescued: lie flat (horizontal), avoid standing (orthostatic collapse risk)
Handle victim gently; remove wet clothing; insulate
Begin CPR if no pulse — cold cardiac arrest is potentially survivable with rewarming
Activate emergency services immediately; cold water casualties need hospital rewarming
